100% agree.  When you think about that statement, it's crazy.  How we would rather hear someone talk about a Star Wars TV show then actually watch it.  Sad part is that it's not just this show.  

I can not understand the stories they choose to go with.  If they were to take a dozen or so actual fans and have them come up with an outline and basic plot for a series, they would have huge hits on their hands.  They refuse to give the people what they want, what they crave.  Hollywood changed and they refuse to listen to anyone who explains why they are failing, instead they double down every time.... and they lose again. 
 
They keep failing over and over, and these same people keep making the same bad choices and seem rewarded for it. It's literal insanity from the outside looking in.  These franchises are well established and making a show that fits in that universe should be simple, very simple.  I have never seen more failures for TV  and movies in my life, and I'm 6 decades in!   This really is not hard to fix, but they keep digging their hole, deaf and blind to anyone who tries to get them to back out.  Almost all of it is just one big waste.

replies.author: @nathancollins1715
replies.text: I don't even understand why the sand line is cringe. He's responding to a comment Padme made about lying on a sandy beach with her friends. For Padme, the thought of sand brings back warm memories of friendship and luxury. For Anakin, sand isn't something you can just take a vacation to and then leave, it's an ever-present reminder of living in a horrible place with horrible conditions against his will for half his childhood. And you can see the effect this has on Padme in the film. She looks down in shame at having been so insensitive as to essentially brag about her privileged upbringing to an ex-slave. Anakin notices this and tries to bridge the gap with physical contact which leads to their first kiss. The scene works really well to highlight their different upbringings but makes the point that their compassion for each other transcends their pasts, and it does it all in only a few lines.

replies.author: @Darkstar-se6wc
replies.text: Didn’t that used to be the writers’ job?

replies.author: @DigOBag
replies.text:
​@Darkstar-se6wcI used to think so, lol. If you're interested to know I finished the rough draft for The Acolyte last month. I titled it Acolyte, because I suck at naming things. 

It features two brothers, Dave(Osha), and Frank(May), raised by their father, Oliver (Smillow Ren), they're also not twins, Dave is 2 years older than Frank. 

No more witches, no more thread counting. They know about the jedi and they're not hiding. The jedi only arrive a year after Frank is born to study the planet, and only discover Oliver and his sons by accident. 

I wrote the jedi characters to not be all good and who'll blur, and in this instance, erase the lines between right and wrong altogether. 

They didn't specifically come for the boys, but idk how jedi find force sensitive people, so I wrote them to be explorers of the galaxy and if they find someone with the force, they attempt to recruit them. 

The Jedi try to recruit Frank, but Dave is considered to have to small a connection with the force to be a candidate for Jedi Academy. Oliver refuses the Jedi. Sohl(Sol), (I did say I suck at naming things), who is more like Scar from the Lion King, manipulates Torvin(Torbin) into helping him take Frank. 

The conflict is largely the same afterwards, Oliver defends his sons against Sohl and Torvin, and is initially winning, but becomes overwhelmed when Outdara(Indara) and Hal Naka(Kelnacca), arrive to protect their friends. Oliver is left 4 dead, but he does recover. I forgot to mention, the Jedi largely ignore Dave, because he's too young to intervene, and I didn't have the heart to write the Xing of a child.

There is a 17 year time jump. 

The story begins with each chapter swapping between, what happened, and how Dave Xs the jedi that were involved (I did it this way to show the results of Daves training during the 17 year time gap), except that Oliver Xs Hal Naka and tortures Hal Nakas apprentice into madness.  

before switching to Frank's PoV, who, after 17 years is a full on Jedi and considers, Annabelle, Hal Naka and Sohl to be some of his closest friends and allies. Torvin too ashamed of what he was convinced to do, exiles himself for it and never knew Frank. 

Sohl and Frank end up investigating the Xing of Jedi, and eventually discover who is behind it. 

There is more too it, but I've written enough here. I'm not a writer (I'm just a janitor from Portland), and I've never been particularly fond of Star Wars. I do recognize the OT is good storytelling, however, and that The Acolyte is basically the opposite of that.

In the old books Luke took rations with him and was sleeping in his x-wing when he had to travel similar distances...

replies.text: Problem here is.....their writing is so scatterbrained they didn't setup anything properly.  Darth Headband actually admitted in interviews that they had major delays do to re-filming scenes due to constant re-writes.  This took so long to film that Daphne Keen (Strong Female #2) gets noticeably older.  Yeah... filming wrapped when she was around 17 or 18, but started when she was more like 15, so in some scenes she's visibly older.  This is mostly due to being incompetent hacks and not actually writing properly.  Probably also why some effects look cheap... they were rushed due to delays caused by reshoots.

replies.author: @GideonRavenor712
replies.text:
You'd think it they'd have progressed tech to where eyewitness testimony is not a prime factor in determining guilt.

But then again, this show didn't think much about how ANY of the logic within it is supposed to work:

-Open flames in a vacuum and other ludicrous space physics and dynamics abound.
-Hamster-man literally just being an entity for the plot to progress the way they want.
-Using basically a pocket-sand trick to try and evade THREE Jedi, one of whom is a Master, another an admittedly incompetent but supposed Knight, and a conversely overpowered Padawan (The only irony there is both of them die).
-The entire impetus for what happens to all the Witches, where the guy who most wanted to leave and got mind-fucked decides to run back to grab the Twins because apparently what they had already gotten wasn't enough even though it logically should have been Sol, not him to make that jump
-Sith Twin fights harder to NOT get arrested even though her entire motivation flip after a grand total of two episodes was to turn herself in (Oh yea, both Twins really show they care a lot about their ideologies and totally aren't just going on them on whims, right?), because apparently being framed for a 3rd murder on top of two she confirmedly did was just a step too far
-Tease Darth Plagueis for no reason other than a second season hook (And undermines any honesty in the motivation Smylo had)
-Waste two episodes with half of each being a repeat of the other.

author: @Grand_Admiral_Thrawn2630
text: I’m not trying to defend the Acolyte in any way, shape, or form, but the reason Smilo-Ren’s helmet shorts out lightsabers is that it’s made either of or with Cortosis, which is an element that existed in the old expanded universe, appearing in games and books alike. Cortosis is also extremely rare, more so than Beskar (which is also lightsaber resistant). I personally don’t mind Cortosis because I grew up playing games and reading the books where it was involved, but I know some fans hated it even back then.

author: @redthefedorashark
text:
33:35
Quite an interesting detail, a material able to temporarily short-circuit a lightsaber - cortosis - exists since old legends and (somehow) still lives in new canon. But I doubt writers or whoever else decided it's a good idea to headbutt a lightsaver did it with cortosis in mind.

author: @evanlayne829
text:
For the headbutting that shuts down lightsabers... Disney revived one of the MANY things they claimed to be getting rid of... unless it's convenient for them. In this case, Cortosis, a brittle ore that can temporarily disable lightsabers, but is practically useless against a detonator. I will give Disney credit for remembering this material, and what it can do, and even how they implemented it in the battle... but WHY was it not used CONSISTANTLY through the battle?!? Also, be sure to check out Shadaversity's breakdown of the lightsaber fight, and you will realize that Nic Gillard (the one that choreographed the original and prequal series' lightsaber duels) was a MILLION times better.

Oh yeah, Lightsaber whips were also supposed to disappear; so that's number 2 out of many.
